title: One School, One Nurse Act of 2025
short_title: One School, One Nurse Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill creates a program where the Department of Education gives money to school districts to hire more nurses. The goal is to make sure every elementary and secondary school has at least one full-time nurse working there. School districts that need the most help and those that promise to hire nurses from groups that don't have many people working in public health will get priority for the money. The Department of Education will also make rules about how many nurses each school should have based on how many students go there.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Education and Workforce, and in addition to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the One School, One Nurse Act of two thousand twenty-five.

sections.title: Section 2  Purpose and Findings
sections.text: The purpose of this law is to help states and local school districts make sure that every elementary and secondary school has at least one full-time registered nurse on staff. It also aims to maintain the right number of nurses compared to students by helping schools recruit, hire, and keep registered nurses. Congress has found several important facts. The American Academy of Pediatrics says every elementary and secondary school should have at least one full-time registered nurse on staff. During the two thousand fifteen to two thousand sixteen school year, only about half of all public schools had a full-time registered nurse and nearly twenty percent did not have any nurse on staff. Schools with more students who qualify for free or reduced-price lunch had fewer school nurses. In two thousand nineteen, roughly six percent of children in the United States did not have health insurance. For children who do not have health care coverage, a school nurse is often a very important source of health services. Studies show that schools with a full-time registered nurse have many benefits including fewer student absences, students spending more time in class, more accurate medical records, higher vaccination rates, fewer student pregnancies, and better health outcomes for students with asthma or diabetes. School nurses play a very important role in helping to manage the ongoing physical, emotional, mental, and social health needs of students, conduct health screenings, help with vaccinations and making sure students meet vaccination requirements, reduce burdens on teachers and other school staff, and support a positive and healthy school environment.

sections.title: Section 3  One School, One Nurse Grant Program
sections.text: No later than twelve months after this law is passed, the Secretary of Education must create a program to give grants on a competitive basis to eligible organizations to carry out specific activities. Grants given under this program will last for five years. An eligible organization that wants a grant must submit an application to the Secretary at the time, in the way, and with the information that the Secretary requires. The application must include a needs assessment that shows there are ongoing shortages of school nurses or ongoing shortages of full-time school nurses. It must describe the health and wellness needs of the students served by the organization. It must include a complete plan for how grant money will be used to address the nurse shortages described in the needs assessment, including how these activities will address the health and wellness needs and how the money will be used to make sure the organization will continue to employ and keep school nurses after the grant period ends. The application must also describe how the organization will focus on recruiting people from the communities it serves and from groups that are not well represented in public health jobs, and how it will track progress in meeting any specific hiring goals. When giving out grants, the Secretary must give priority to eligible organizations that are a high-need local school district or are a partnership between a state education agency and a group of high-need local school districts, and that describe and set specific hiring goals toward increasing the number of school nurses who are from groups that are not well represented in public health jobs. Grant money must be used by an eligible organization to do one or more of these activities: recruit and hire school nurses, provide resources needed to change part-time school nurse positions at elementary or secondary schools into full-time school nurse positions, support keeping school nurses by increasing salaries, and maintain the recommended number of nurses compared to students. Each organization that gets a grant must submit an annual report to the Secretary for each year of the grant. The report must include a summary of the organization's progress in employing at least one full-time registered nurse at each elementary and secondary school it serves and maintaining the recommended number of nurses compared to students. It must include data on the number and percentage of full-time and part-time school nurses, broken down by major racial and ethnic groups and gender, employed at each school served by the organization. It must also include a summary of any progress made in addressing the health and wellness needs identified in the needs assessment as a result of activities done with grant money. No later than twelve months after this law is passed, the Secretary must work with the Secretary of Health and Human Services to create regulations that specify the recommended number of nurses compared to students for elementary and secondary schools, provide guidance and other technical help to eligible organizations to achieve and maintain these ratios, and define what groups are not well represented in public health jobs for the purposes of this law. The Secretary must also work with the Secretary of Health and Human Services and the Secretary of Labor to define what full-time means for school nurses under this law. In this law, eligible entity means a local school district or a partnership between a state education agency and a group of local school districts in the state that is created for the purposes of getting a grant under this law. High-need local educational agency has the same meaning as given in the Higher Education Act. Local educational agency and state educational agency have the same meanings as given in the Elementary and Secondary Education Act. School nurse means a registered nurse who is employed by a school or local school district or state or state education agency and is qualified under state law to provide assessment, diagnosis, counseling, educational, therapeutic, and other health services to meet student needs. Secretary means the Secretary of Education.
